findings.description: list(reader) materializes the whole CSV as a list-of-lists (3-5x raw size in Python object overhead) and keeps it alive while a second full list of Record objects is built (plus a rows[1:] pointer-list copy); iterate the reader lazily instead.
count_rows loads the entire file into memory via readlines() just to len() it; use a context manager and sum(1 for _ in f) for O(1) memory.
File opened without with/close, so the descriptor's lifetime depends on CPython refcounting and leaks under exceptions or non-refcounting interpreters.

stream_valid advertises Iterator semantics but calls parse(), which fully materializes rows and records before the first yield — O(n) memory and full-parse first-item latency, defeating the streaming contract.
Record dataclass lacks slots=True, so each instance carries a __dict__ that inflates bulk-ingest memory roughly 2-3x at scale.
find_by_id is an O(n) linear scan per call — fine once, O(n*m) if any caller loops over IDs; build a {id: record} dict for repeated lookups (no callers in scope, hence low confidence).
The header index dict is computed and never used — dead work with negligible cost (its non-use is the logic hazard reported separately).
os.makedirs runs on every CsvIngestor construction — a redundant syscall if instances are created per-request.

Header is validated as an order-insensitive set but fields are read positionally (row[0..3]), so a reordered-but-valid header silently mis-maps columns, and one malformed row aborts the whole batch via uncaught ValueError/IndexError — deferred to critic-logic.
count_rows returns -1 for an empty file and counts physical lines rather than CSV records (quoted embedded newlines miscounted) — deferred to critic-logic.
Exports sharing a basename silently overwrite each other (mode 'w', no uniquing), a collision/concurrency hazard — deferred to critic-logic.
